coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots for application in acetone and dopamine sensors and multicolor cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yushan
Li, Wei
Wu, Peng
Ma, Chunhui
Wu, Xueyun
Xu, Mingcong
Luo, Sha
Xu, Zhou
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots (NB-CQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, borax, and p-phenylenediamine. The synthesized NB-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra, dynamic light scattering, ultraviolet-visible absorption and fluorescence spectra to characterize their surface morphology, crystal structure, functional groups and elemental composition, and optical properties. It was found that N and B species were efficiently doped into the carbon framework of the dots, the N- and B-Containing groups formed on the surface of NB-CQDs during the hydrothermal reaction. The element doping and surface functionalization of NB-CQDs efficiently modulated their physicochemical properties. The NB-CQDs were nearly mono-disperse with an average particle diameter of 3.53 nm, and the excitation/emission wavelengths were 360/490 nm, respectively. The NB-CQDs showed regular photoluminescence (PL) emission under different excitation wavelengths, and possessed pH-dependent and solvent polarity-dependent PL properties. In addition, the NB-CQDs and a NB-CQD/poly(vinyl alcohol) composite film showed good PL responses to acetone solution and vapor. A PL sensor for the determination of acetone solution with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.54 μM (R2 = 0.9956) was then designed. The NB-CQDs were also used for the determination of dopamine. The PL intensity of the NB-CQDs was inversely proportional to the concentration of dopamine in the range of 0.1–70 μM (R2 = 0.9958). The LOD of dopamine was 11 nM, and PL quenching occurred via a static quenching mechanism. Additionally, these NB-CQDs can be used for multicolor cellular imaging and showed good uptake by Hepg2 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Heteroatom doping
Acetone sensor
Dopamine detection
Multicolor cellular imaging
Hydrothermal carbonization

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid otherkwds primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cellular toxicity test and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (inset) size distribution histogram of the NB-CQDs; (B) HRTEM image of a single NB-CQD; (C) Size distribution of NB-CQDs measured by DLS; (D) Apparent zeta potential (ζ) of NB-CQDs in aqueous solution.
FT-IR spectra of NB-CQDs and CA.
(A) XPS survey spectra of NB-CQDs; (B) High-resolution C1s peaks; (C) High-resolution O1s peaks; (D) High-resolution B1s peaks.

(A) UV–vis absorption and PLE spectrum (PL emission at 490 nm), the inset (a) shows a digital photograph of NB-CQDs under 365 nm UV light, inset (b) presents the UV–vis absorption spectra; (B) PL emission spectrum of NB-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (C) PL emission spectrum of N,B-CQDs at different pH values, inset shows PL emission intensity at different pH values; (D) PL emission spectrum of NB-CQD aqueous solution mixed with different solvents (excited at 360 nm), the inset (a) shows the PL emission of NB-CQD aqueous solution, the inset (b) shows a digital photograph of NB-CQD aqueous solution and AC-CQDs under 365 nm UV light; (E) Energy level diagram for the tunable PL emission of NB-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) PL emission spectrum of NB-CQDs in the presence of various concentrations of AC (from bottom to top, 0, 0.2, 1, 20, 40, 80, 200, 400, 600, 800 μM, excited at 360 nm); (B) The relationship between PL emission intensity and AC concentration; (C) Schematic of AC vapor sensing by NB-CQD/PVA composite film, the inset is NB-CQD/PVA composite film; (D–F) PL microscopy images of NB-CQD/PVA composite film under excitation by UV light: under initial conditions, treated with normal air, and treated with AC vapor, respectively.

(A) PL emission spectrum of NB-CQDs in the presence of various concentrations of DA (from top to bottom, 0, 0.1, 20, 40, 70, 100, 180, 240, 320 μM, excited at 360 nm), the inset displays the relationship between quenching efficiency and DA concentration; (B) The relationship between PL emission intensity and DA concentration; (C) The selectivity of NB-CQDs against various small organic molecules (the concentration of organic molecules was 1000 μM).

(A) Fluorescence decay curves of NB-CQDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NB-CQDs in the presence of various concentrations of DA, the inset presents the UV–vis absorption spectrum in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm; (C) Model of DA detection mechanism.

(A) Cytotoxicity studies of the NB-CQDs (24 h posttreatment) by the MTT assay for Hepg2 cells; (B) bright-field microscopy image of Hepg2 cells (a), and fluorescence microscopy images of Hepg2 cells (b–f) incubated with 75 μg mL−1 NB-CQDs for 6 h; the fluorescence images were obtained under excitation at 315, 365, 445, 485, 515 nm, respectively. Each scale bar represents 40 μm.
Comparison of current and reported CQD-based PL sensors for DA detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon nanoparticles and Fe3+ quenching system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded carbon dots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: DA aptamer labeled carbon dots and nano-graphite.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots for application in acetone and dopamine sensors and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots (NB-CQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, borax, and p-phenylenediamine.

The synthesized NB-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra, dynamic light scattering, ultraviolet-visible absorption and fluorescence spectra to characterize their surface morphology, crystal structure, functional groups and elemental composition, and optical properties. It was found that N and B species were efficiently doped into the carbon framework of the dots, the N- and B-Containing groups formed on the surface of NB-CQDs during the hydrothermal reaction. The element doping and surface functionalization of NB-CQDs efficiently modulated their physicochemical properties. The NB-CQDs were nearly mono-disperse with an average particle diameter of 3.53 nm, and the excitation/emission wavelengths were 360/490 nm, respectively. The NB-CQDs showed regular photoluminescence (PL) emission under different excitation wavelengths, and possessed pH-dependent and solvent polarity-dependent PL properties. In addition, the NB-CQDs and a NB-CQD/poly(vinyl alcohol) composite film showed good PL responses to acetone solution and vapor. A PL sensor for the determination of acetone solution with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.54 μM (R2 = 0.9956) was then designed. The NB-CQDs were also used for the determination of dopamine. The PL intensity of the NB-CQDs was inversely proportional to the concentration of dopamine in the range of 0.1–70 μM (R2 = 0.9958). The LOD of dopamine was 11 nM, and PL quenching occurred via a static quenching mechanism. Additionally, these NB-CQDs can be used for multicolor cellular imaging and showed good uptake by Hepg2 cells.

ce:keyword.ce:text: nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) –doping heteroatoms have attracted much attention because of their novel optical properties different from pure CQDs, and potential applications in analysis and detection, vivo imaging and cellular labeling, and light-emitting diodes, et al [1–7]. Similar to the n-type and p-type doping of semiconductors [8,9], the heteroatom doping of CQDs can change the properties due to doping of electron donors or acceptors into their structures. Among the numerous heteroatoms, N and B have similar atomic structures to neighboring C in the periodic table. Thus, doping with N and B can efficiently modulate the physicochemical properties of CQDs. For instance, Yang et al. reported the hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped CQDs using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine. Doping nitrogen greatly increased the fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs to 80%. The N-doped CQDs could be used to detect Fe3+ [10]. Fang et al. reported the electrochemical preparation of B-doped CQDs. Doping B resulted in strong photoluminescence (PL) emission, because of the CQDs’ specific interaction with the surrounding medium (borax solution). The B-doped CQDs could be used to detect Al3+, and as a biomarker for cellular imaging [11]. Huang et al. synthesized NB-CQDs by the one-pot solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine and formylphenylboronic acid. The NB-CQDs showed high affinity for binding nucleic acids. The presence of B was beneficial to the absorption of single-stranded DNA, while the presence of N promoted the release of single-stranded DNA. These properties were exploited to detect DNA of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) [12].

Acetone (AC) is a common laboratory and industrial solvent that is miscible with water at all concentrations. This efficient solubility in water can be harmful to the human body. Extended exposure to AC can lead to skin dryness, dermatitis, and throat and eye irritation. Hence, searching for sensitive, effective, low-cost and rapid detection methods of AC in environment is necessary and significant In the past few years, many AC sensors have been reported, including a Au and Pd-decorated WO3 nanorod AC sensor [13], a pure and Pt-loaded gamma iron oxide AC sensor [14], and a polypyrrole and polyaniline conducting-polymer AC gas sensor [15]. Jana et al. recently reported that AC can quench the PL of CQDs, and thought that quenching occurred presumably due to the disruption of radiative recombination along with the hindrance in quantum confinement of the emissive energy trap to the particle surface [16]. Das et al. reported that S and N co-doped CQDs showed good PL detection of AC. This was attributed to the effective electronic interaction between the CQDs and AC. The doped CQDs could be used to detect ketones in blood and urine samples [17]. Despite this progress, there are few other reports of AC sensors based on CQDs, especially for detecting AC vapor. Therefore, the development of CQDs-based AC sensor is necessary. Dopamine (DA), one kind of catecholamine neurotransmitter in human brain, the abnormal changes of its concentration will result in the emergence of clinic illness, such as Parkinson’s disease and Huntington's disease [18,19]. How to detect the DA simply and quickly is an important topic concerned by the researchers all over the world. Typical DA detection methods include spectrophotometry [20], chemiluminescence [21], liquid chromatography [22], and electrochemical methods [23]. Compared with above technologies, the fluorescence detection is one kind of easy and efficient method. Up to now, many fluorescent probes have been developed to detect DA, including molecular [24] and quantum dot fluorescent probes [25,26]. Traditional fluorescent probes are usually based on organic molecules or heavy metal-containing quantum dots. In contrast, CQD-based fluorescent probes are effective, low-toxicity, readily surface-functionalized, and stable for detecting DA [27–29]. The specific o-hydroxyl structure of DA can form stable complexes with boric acid species [30,31]. Thus, boric acid-functionalized CQDs are a potential candidate for DA sensors. In addition, nanomaterials are expected to form the basis of high-performing contrast agents for imaging, drug delivery vehicles, and biomarker sensors for early disease detection [32–37]. Benefitting from CQDs’ favorable characteristics, such as strong PL emission in the visible spectral range, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, strong resistance to photobleaching, and ease of synthesis and surface modification, they have been used for bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery [38–40]. Research into multicolor cellular imaging will provide a platform for the determination of intracellular DA.

Herein, a hydrothermal carbonization method for the synthesis of N, B-CQDs was reported. The NB-CQDs showed regular PL emission behavior under different excitation wavelengths, and possessed pH-dependent and solvent polarity-dependent PL. A PL mechanism for the NB-CQDs was proposed based on these results. The NB-CQDs were applied as PL sensors for detecting AC and DA, and their application for Hepg2 multicolor cellular imaging was also reported.

CA, p-phenylenediamine, borax (Na2B4O7·10H2O), ascorbic acid, azithromycin, DA, uric acid, glucose, glucosamine, melamine, cysteine, tyrosine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, valine, and phenylalanine were purchased from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, P.R. China). AC, methanol, ethanol, N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, P.R. China). Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, P.R. China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 100 mM, pH = 7.4), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, High Glucose), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and trypsinase were purchased from Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, P. R. China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

NB-CQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal carbonization of CA, p-phenylenediamine, and borax. The preparation process of NB-CQDs is simple as shown in Fig. 1 . Typically, 1 g each of CA, p-phenylenediamine, and borax were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a beaker under stirring, then the mixture was transferred to a poly(p-phenylene)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature in air. Next, the crude product was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then subjected to dialysis in water (500 Da molecular weight cutoff) for about 24 h, the synthesized CQDs are denoted NB-CQDs For comparison, CQDs prepared via a similar procedure with only CA (i.e. no p-phenylenediamine and borax) are denoted O-CQDs. CQDs prepared via a similar procedure with only p-phenylenediamine and borax (i.e. no CA) are denoted PB-CQDs.

Typically, 3 g of PVA was mixed with 60 mL of deionized water under continuous stirring in a water bath until the PVA completely dissolved. Then, 0.5 mL of NB-CQD aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) was added, and the mixture was stirred for another 1 h to ensure the NB-CQDs were completely dispersed. The well-dispersed mixture (3 mL) was conditioned to a rectangular plastic container (3 cm in length and 3 cm in width). Finally, the solidified films were vacuum-dried overnight and kept in a dryer containing silica gel.

The effect of various solvents on the PL behavior of the NB-CQDs was investigated. Typically, 2 mL of NB-CQDs aqueous solution (Abs = 0.1) was mixed with 2 mL of one of six kinds of solvents: water, ethanol, methanol, AC, DMSO, or DMF. The resulting solutions were reacted for 1 min, then measured the PL emission of NB-CQDs mixed solutions. The PL detection of AC by the NB-CQDs was then studied based on the results of the solvent effect tests. AC detection experiments were performed in deionized water. Different concentrations of AC were added to the NB-CQD aqueous solution, with the final AC concentration ranging from 0 μM to 800 μM. The solutions were reacted for 1 min before PL measurement. All samples were excited at 360 nm, and the PL intensities were recorded at a wavelength of 490 nm As for AC vapor sensor, a composite film of NB-CQD/PVA (1 cm × 1 cm) and 20 mL of AC (50%) were put into the vacuum desiccator. For the control experiment, a NB-CQD/PVA composite film (1 cm × 1 cm) and 20 mL of water were put into the vacuum desiccator. The PL images of NB-CQD/PVA were measured after 2 h.

The detection of DA was performed in PBS solution (100 mM, pH = 7.4). Typically, different concentrations of DA were added to the NB-CQD PBS solution. The final concentration of DA ranged from 0 μM to 320 μM. The solutions reacted for 20 min before PL measurement. All samples were excited at 360 nm, and the PL intensities were recorded at the wavelength of 490 nm. In order to exploring the selectivity of detection for DA, different small organic molecules including ascorbic acid, azithromycin, uric acid, glucose, glucosamine, melamine, cysteine, tyrosine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, valine, and phenylalanine were investigated in place of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity test and multicolor cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the NB-CQDs against Hepg2 cells was evaluated by a standard MTT assay. Typically, Hepg2 cells were seeded into a 96-well cell culture plate in DMEM at a density of 1000–10000 cells per 100 μL in each well with 10% FBS and 5% CO2 and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the culture medium was replaced with 200 μL of DMEM containing different doses of NB-CQDs, and the cells were cultured for another 24 h. Then, 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added to each well. The cells were incubated for a further 4 h, followed by the removal of the culture medium with MTT; 150 μL of DMSO was then added to each well. The resulting mixture was shaken for 10 min at room temperature. The absorbance of MTT at 490 nm was measured using an automatic ELISA analyzer (MD Spectra Max M3, USA). Control experiments without NB-CQDs were also performed. Each experiment was conducted three times, and the data were averaged.

The obtained NB-CQDs were used for cellular imaging of Hepg2. Typically, Hepg2 cells were seeded into 6-well culture plates at a density of 10,000 per well in DMEM containing 10% FBS and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. After the removal of DMEM, a mixture of NB-CQDs (75 μg mL−1) in DMEM was added to each well for 6 h of incubation. Finally, the cells were washed twice with PBS to remove extracellular NB-CQDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. Subsequently, the cells were observed by inverted fluorescence microscope (Nano Focus, Germany).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan), at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) in the range 400-4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using an Escalab 250 XI spectrometer (Thermo, USA) with Al-K α radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV). Binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8 eV. The hydrodynamic particle size and apparent zeta potential were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1950 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescent lifetimes were measured on an FLSP980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments Co., UK) with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. Fluorescence images were obtained using a Leica DM IL LED (Leica, Germany) inverted fluorescence microscope.

PL emission spectra of the NB-CQDs, O-CQDs, and PB-CQDs excited at 360 nm, and UV–vis absorption spectra of these samples, are shown in Fig. S1. The O-CQDs showed a weak PL emission peak around 430 nm, nevertheless, the NB-CQDs and P B-CQDs both showed markedly red-shifted and stronger PL emission peaks at 490 nm. This indicated that the structural network of the CQDs may have been altered by B and N doping. The increase of PL intensity at 490 nm indicated a possible transfer of energy/electrons from B and N to the fluorophore of the CQDs. In addition, the PB-CQDs showed similar PL emission behavior with N B-CQDs, except for weaker PL peak, which confirmed that CA acting as carbon source played a significant role in CQDs formation progress. The hydrothermal carbonization synthesis of the CQDs involved dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation of the raw materials. The abundant carboxyl groups of CA benefited the formation of carbon cores and surface functionalization for NB-CQDs, which resulted in PL intensity increasing [10,41–44]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the PB-CQDs and O-CQDs showed weaker, broader absorption bands than the NB-CQDs. This further confirm that the NB-CQDs can be prepared reasonably by current method. Fig. 2 A, B shows the TEM (HRTEM) images and a size distribution histogram of the NB-CQDs, which had an average diameter of 3.53 nm and size distribution of 1.2–6.4 nm. The HRTEM image of a single N, B-CQDs (inset) reveals the high crystallinity of the NB-CQDs with a lattice-spacing distance of approximately 0.206 nm and it is close to that of the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [45,46]. The slightly smaller lattice constant for the NB-CQDs was a consequence of B and N atoms being doped into the conjugated carbon backbone, resulting in a somewhat disordered structure. The DLS data in Fig. 2C, meanwhile, shows a size distribution in the range of 1.1–5.9 nm. The smaller size derived by DLS can be attributed to the better dispersion of NB-CQDs in water, whilst agglomeration may have occurred during the drying process in TEM sample preparation.

FT-IR and XPS spectra were used to analyze the surface states and elemental compositions of the NB-CQDs. Fig. 3 shows FT-IR spectra of the NB-CQDs and CA. The peaks at 3358 cm−1 and 2932 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O/NH and CH groups, respectively. The peak at 1579 cm−1 was associated with the CC stretching vibration. Its presence indicated the formation of unsaturated CC bonds within the carbon cores, which was consistent with the result for the HRTEM lattice spacing. The peak at 1199 cm−1 was attributed to the CO vibration of COC groups. Peaks at 1701 cm−1, 1516 cm−1, and 1294 cm−1 were assigned to the typical stretching modes of NCO, CN, and C-N-C bonds. The NH bending vibration was observed at 833 cm−1. The FT-IR peaks at 1397 cm−1, 1168 cm−1, and 1120 cm−1 of the NB-CQDs were attributed to B-O stretching, BOH bending, and BC stretching, respectively [47,48]. The FT-IR results confirmed that N and B-Containing groups had formed via hydrothermal carbonization, and that N and B were doped into the framework of the NB-CQDs.

The XPS full-scan spectrum (Fig. 4 A) shows that NB-CQDs contain C, O, N and B elements, and the contents of the four elements were calculated to be 69.50%, 22.79%, 6.48% and 1.23%, respectively. The C1s (284.8 eV) spectrum (Fig. 4B) was deconvoluted into six peaks at 288.8 eV (OC = O), 288.2 eV (CO), 285.9 eV (CO), 285.1 eV (CN), 284.8 eV (CC/C-C), and 283.9 eV (CB). This suggested that there were a large amount of oxygen-containing groups presenting at the surface of the NB-CQDs, which was consistent with the FT-IR results. The N1s (399.65 eV) spectrum (Fig. 4C) of the NB-CQDs was deconvoluted into three peaks at 400.3 eV, 399.6 eV, and 398.9 eV, which were assigned to NH, NC, and C = NC groups, respectively. The B1s (192.12 eV) spectrum (Fig. 4D) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 192.7 eV (B-O) and 192.1 eV (B-C) [28,49]. The XPS survey spectra demonstrated that N and B-Containing groups had formed within NB-CQDs.

The apparent zeta potential (ζ) of NB-CQDs in aqueous solution was measured to be negative, around -9.9 mV (Fig. 3D). This indicated the presence of negatively charged moieties (such as COOH, CONH, OH and boric acid structure) on the surface of NB-CQDs.

The NB-CQD solution showed blue-green PL emission under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5 A, inset). To explore the optical properties of the NB-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption and PL emission behavior of the NB-CQDs were further investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NB-CQDs in Fig. 5A shows a typical absorption peak at 243 nm, assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Moreover, the two absorption peaks at 281 and 350 nm were identified as n-π* absorption bands [50]. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of the NB-CQDs in Fig. 5A was recorded at the wavelength of maximum PL emission (490 nm). The PLE spectrum showed two peaks at 273 nm and 360 nm, which were consistent with the UV–vis absorption bands [51].

The PL emission behavior of the NB-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths was investigated. Fig. 5B shows that the NB-CQDs exhibited dual emission behavior when the excitation wavelength was in the range of 260–280 nm. The two emission peaks were 390 nm (blue) and 490 nm (blue-green, respectively, and these peaks were excitation-independent. The NB-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL emission behavior when the excitation wavelength was in the range of 320–400 nm. The maximum emission wavelength was about 490 nm, when the excitation wavelength was about 360 nm, thus, 360 nm was selected as the maximum excitation wavelength in the following experiments. The excitation-independent PL emission may have originated from the n-σ* (blue) and n-π* (blue-green) transitions. Because the PL emission at this time is a single transition process that requiring a fixed amount of energy, the PL emission is excitation-independent. When the excitation wavelength exceeded 400 nm, there was an obvious red-shift in emission peak, indicating excitation-dependent PL emission. This excitation-dependent PL emission resulted from the abundant surface states, and demonstrated the multicolor properties of the NB-CQDs [52].

A mechanism for the PL emission is proposed in Fig. 5E. The PL of NB-CQDs involves free zigzag sites with a carbene triplet ground state described as a combination of σ and π, with the multiplicity attributed to the energy difference (ΔE <1.5 eV) between the σ and π orbitals. The peak at 273 nm (4.54 eV) is believed to result from the n-σ* transition, while the peak at 360 nm (3.44 eV) is ascribed to the n-π* transition. The energy difference between the σ* and π* orbitals is 1.1 eV, which is within the value expected for triplet carbenes, suggesting that the assignment of the two transitions is reasonable. This indicated the reasonability of the transitions to generate PL emission as the decay from LUMO to π, with the appearance of non-irradiative recombination [53].

The PL emission behavior of the NB-CQDs in different pH conditions was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5C. 1 mol·L−1 NaOH and 3 mol·L−1 HCl were used to adjust the pH of the NB-CQD aqueous solution. Although the PL emission intensity of the NB-CQDs decreased under strongly acidic and alkaline conditions, the PL emission remained relatively stable over a wide pH range of 4–10 [47]. High pH (>10) increased the negative charge density accumulated on the surface and suppressed the π-π* and n-π* transitions, leading to a decrease in PL emission intensity. The maximum emission peak also blue-shifted from 490 nm to 460 nm at low pH (≤4). This indicated that low pH perturbed the PL emission centers of the NB-CQDs. The blue-shifted PL at low pH may have resulted from the binding of protons to emissive sites of the NB-CQDs. The pH-responsive properties suggested that the NB-CQDs have potential application in pH sensing.

According to previous reports, B-doped CQDs exhibited a rich diversity of PL properties as a result of their specific interaction with the surrounding media [11]. Therefore, NB-CQD aqueous solutions were mixed with non-aqueous solvents including AC, ethanol, methanol, DMF, and DMSO, to study their solvent effects. After mixing the NB-CQD aqueous solution with other weakly polar solvents, especially AC (referred to as AC-CQDs), different PL emission centers appeared, and the PL emission intensity increased noticeably. In mixtures containing AC, ethanol, methanol, DMF, or DMSO, the intensity of the original emission peak at 490 nm gradually decreased with increasing amount of added solvent. Corresponding with this decrease, two PL emission centers appeared at 436 nm and 464 nm. Furthermore, the PL emission intensity increased with decreasing solvent polarity. The PLE spectra for the AC-CQDs were monitored under the wavelengths of the PL emissions at 436 nm and 464 nm, and the PLE spectra both showed the same peak around 360 nm, as shown in Fig. S2(A). In contrast to reported studies, the NB-CQDs showed similar PL emission behavior in both hydrogen bonding and aprotic solvents, as shown in Fig. 5D [54]. This result could potentially be exploited to develop a polarity sensor.

The PL emission behavior of the AC-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths was also studied, as shown in Fig. S2(B). The AC-CQDs showed similar PL emission behavior to that of the NB-CQDs. Specifically, excitation-independent PL emission was observed at excitation wavelengths of 320–420 nm, and maximum PL emission intensity was observed when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm. Red-shifted PL emission was observed when the excitation wavelength exceeded 400 nm, indicating excitation-dependent emission. The abundant surface states contributed to this excitation-dependent emission.

Two explanations for the solvent effect are proposed. On the one hand, solvent molecules could coordinate with the NB-CQDs, causing surface passivation. This would increase the PL emission intensity and change the emission centers [55]. On the other hand, the large blue-shift in PL emission might have been caused by the strong electron-withdrawing ability of B. Therefore, the NB-CQDs would interact with high-electron-density solvents, leading to an increase in PL intensity [56].

A PL sensor for AC was developed based on the enhancement in PL emission intensity. Fig. 6 A shows that the PL emission intensity of the NB-CQDs at 490 nm gradually increased with increasing AC concentration. Fig. 6B shows the ratio of the PL emission intensity versus the AC concentration, and a good linear correlation of R2 = 0.9956 versus the concentration of AC was found in the range of 1–200 μM, giving a linear regression equation of F = 1323.0242 + 1.0026 [AC concentration]. The LOD for AC was determined to be 0.54 μM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. AC is a highly volatile solvent, so detecting AC in the vapor phase is perhaps of higher practical value. Thus, the NB-CQDs were applied in a PL sensor to detect AC vapor. A NB-CQD/PVA composite film was prepared (Fig. 6C), and showed weak blue-green emission under excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 6D). After exposure to AC vapor in a vacuum desiccator, the PL emission intensity of the NB-CQD/PVA composite film increased noticeably. The PL emission also changed from blue-green for the NB-CQD/PVA composite film exposed to ambient air, into blue for the film exposed to AC vapor (Fig. 6E, F). This result demonstrated that the NB-CQD/PVA composite film could be used for the visual detection of AC vapor (Fig. S3).

Boric acid species can form stable complexes with cis-diol structures [57]. Thus, the present boric acid-functionalized CQDs are a potential candidate for DA sensing. Boric acid-functionalized CQDs were previously used as a fluorescent sensor to detect o-hydroxyl species [58].

Inspired by the verification of their excellent PL properties, the as-prepared NB-CQDs were used for multicolor cellular imaging of Hepg2 cells. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the MTT assay shows that the NB-CQDs exhibit low toxicity and good biocompatibility, which makes them applicable for in vivo cell imaging and biological labeling. Specifically, more than 78% Hepg2 cells were viable after incubation at a NB-CQD concentration of 200 μg ml−1 for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 9B, confocal images of Hepg2 cells incubated for 6 h in an NB-CQDs concentration of 75 μg ml−1 were recorded in a bright field (Fig. 9B (a)) and under illumination at different wavelengths (Fig. 9B (b–f)). Under illumination, the cells emitted blue, light blue, green, yellow, and red PL. This suggests that the NB-CQDs have good cell permeability and are suitable for live cellular imaging. This finding also provides a platform for the determination of intracellular DA.

NB-CQDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal approach. N and B-Containing groups formed on the surface of NB-CQDs, the N and B atoms were also doped into the framework of the NB-CQDs. The element doping and surface functionalization of NB-CQDs improved their optical properties. The NB-CQDs exhibited regular PL emission behavior under different excitation wavelengths and pH environments, and the PL was also solvent polarity-dependent. The NB-CQDs were an effective fluorescent probe for AC concentrations of 1–200 μM, with an LOD of 0.54 μM. A NB-CQD/PVA composite film was developed for the visual detection of AC vapor. The NB-CQDs were also used for the sensitive PL detection of DA. The PL intensity of the NB-CQDs was inversely proportional to the DA concentration in the range of 0.1–70 μM. The LOD of DA was 11 nM, and quenching occurred via a static mechanism. Finally, the as-prepared NB-CQDs were successfully applied for Hepg2 multicolor cellular imaging. These NB-CQDs showed promising potential to be applied in a wide range of applications, such as water and air quality monitoring, food quality supervision, and biomedical control.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31570567, 31500467), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. 2572017ET02, Nos. 2572018AB02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.075.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots doped with heteroatoms for fluorescent bioimaging: a review
Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
A strong green fluorescent nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detection of nitrite ions based on phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
Multifunctional nitrogedoped carbon dots from maleic anhydride and tetraethylenepentamine via pyrolysis for sensing, adsorbance, and imaging applications
Bottom-up preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with green emission under microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment and their biological imaging
Heavily doped semiconductor nanocrystal quantum dots
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Surrounding media sensitive photoluminescence of boron-doped graphene quantum dots for highly fluorescent dyed crystals, chemical sensing and bioimaging

Boron and nitrogen co-doped single-layered graphene quantum dots: a high-affinity platform for visualizing the dynamic invasion of HIV DNA into living cells through fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Acetone sensing of Au and Pd-decorated WO3 nanorod sensors
Pure and Pt-loaded gamma iron oxide as sensor for detection of sub ppm level of acetone
On the sensitivity of conductimetric acetone gas sensor based on polypyrrole and polyaniline conducting polymers
One pot synthesis of intriguing fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and live cell imaging
Heteroatom doped photoluminescent carbon dots for sensitive detection of acetone in human fluids
Detection of dopamine dynamics in the brain
Dopamine and oxytocin interactions underlying behaviors: potential contributions to behavioral disorders
Aptamer-based colorimetric biosensing of dopamine using unmodified gold nanoparticles
Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer-based detection for microchip electrophoresis

Determination of monoamine neurotransmitters and their metabolites in a mouse brain microdialysate by coupling high-performance liquid chromatography with gold nanoparticle-initiated chemiluminescence
Selective electrochemical detection of dopamine on polyoxometalate‐based metal-organic framework and its composite with reduced graphene oxide
A simple, fast and low-cost turn-on fluorescence method for dopamine detection using in situ reaction
Luminescent ZnO quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine
Adenosine capped QDs based fluorescent sensor for detection of dopamine with high selectivity and sensitivity
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe for dopamine based on the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and dopamine
Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for the detection of dopamine
3-aminophenyl boronic acid-functionalized CuInS2 quantum dots as a near-infrared fluorescence probe for the determination of dopamine
Acrylic polymers with pendant phenylboronic acid moieties as “Turn-off” and “Turn-on” fluorescence solid sensors for detection of dopamine, glucose, and fructose in Water

One-step synthesis, self-assembly and bioimaging applications of adenosine triphosphate containing amphiphilies with aggregation-induced emission feature
Facile synthesis of polymeric fluorescent organic nanoparticles based on the self-polymerization of dopamine for biological imaging
Recent developments in polydopamine: an emerging soft matter for surface modification and biomedical applications
Biocompatible polydopamine fluorescent organic nanoparticles: facile preparation and cell imaging
Polymeric AIE-based nanoprobes for biomedical applications: recent advances and perspectives
Recent advances in carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and biomedical applications
Graphene quantum dots for theranostics and bioimaging
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
Formation mechanism and optimization of highly luminescent N-doped graphene quantum dots
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped and surface-passivated carbon quantum dots with high quantum yield and excellent optical properties
Cutting sp(2) clusters in graphene sheets into colloidal graphene quantum dots with strong green fluorescence
Ultra‐High quantum yield of graphene quantum dots: aromatic‐nitrogen doping and photoluminescence mechanism
Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and boron doped carbon quantum dots with yellow-green emission for sensing Cr(VI), anti-counterfeiting and cell imaging
Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from microcrystalline cellulose for the detection of Fe3+ ions in an acidic environment
Fluorescence quenchometric method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots

graphene quantum dots: facile synthetic method for pristine graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide quantum dots: origin of Blue and Green luminescence
Reduced carbon dots versus oxidized carbon dots: photo- and electrochemiluminescence investigations for selected applications
Acid-Free microwave approach to prepare highly luminescent boron-doped graphene quantum dots for cell imaging
Observation of fluorescence from non-functionalized carbon nanoparticles and its solvent dependent spectroscopy
Preparation of high-quality biocompatible carbon dots by extraction, with new thoughts on the luminescence mechanisms
B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Recognition and sensing of various species using boronic acid derivatives
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the "abnormal" aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement
Green and cost effective synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots for dopamine detection
Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yushan Liu is a graduate student for a PhD degree in Northeast Forestry University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on fabrication and modification of fluorescent functional nano-materials for analytical and bioimaging applications.

Wei Li received his PhD degree in 2011 from Northeast Forestry University, China. His research interest focuses on the fabrication and modification of carbon materials for analytical and catalytic applications.

Peng Wu is a graduate student for a PhD degree in Northeast Forestry University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on optical properties of carbon nano-materials and multi-information fluorescence probe.

Chunhui Ma received her PhD degree in 2011 from Northeast Forestry University, China. Her research interest focuses on the fabrication and modification of carbon materials for analytical applications.
Xueyun Wu is a graduate student for a Master's degree, her research interest focuses on delayed fluorescence nano-materials and analytical applications.
Mingcong Xu is a graduate student for a Master's degree, his research interest focuses on modification of carbon nono-materials.

Sha Luo is a graduate student for a PhD degree in Northeast Forestry University, China. His research interests focus mainly on modification of carbon nono-materials for analytical and catalytic applications

Zhou Xu is a graduate student for a PhD degree in Northeast Forestry University, China. His research interests focus mainly on modification of carbon nanosphere for analytical and bioimaging applications.

Shouxin Liu is a professor in the College of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, China. He was hired as a Yangtze river scholars distinguished professor in 2015. His research interests focus mainly on carbon nano-materials, elec-trochemical sensor and photocatalysis.
